div.head: The German Ambassador (Bernstorff) to Colonel E. M. House This paper bears the notation: “Copy of letter given me by Polk—Nov. 28/16 RL.”
div.p: My Dear Colonel House: On Tuesday I sent a long cable to Berlin as I promised you.

There is no intention on the part of my Government to make any change in the principle of submarine warfare. I am, therefore, sure, that Berlin will be prepared to make all necessary reparation for the mistakes in the Arabia and Marina cases. If these two ships were really not—as the submarine commanders believed them to be—armed transport ships, they certainly came very near it.

I hope the whole question of “armed merchantmen” will not be brought up, but that we can limit the discussion to “armed transports” so that a long controversy is avoided, and I do hope that the President will be satisfied, if my Government goes as far as it can.
I shall call on Mr. Lansing as soon as I receive definite information. If you wish to see me on this subject, please wire or phone and I will come over to New York immediately for a few hours.

What do you think of Lord Derby’s speech?British Under Secretary of State for War. It seems to confirm my opinion, that the Allies could not and would not refuse to talk, and that is all they are to be asked to do. Whether the negotiations would lead to peace, is, of course, quite a different matter, but I am confident that they would.
